python.explanation:
This implements a greedy local search (hill climbing) using adjacent swaps as the neighborhood. At each iteration, all O(n) adjacent swap candidates are evaluated by computing total tardiness after the swap. The swap with the greatest reduction in tardiness is applied. This is a steepest-descent approach within the swap neighborhood.

Computing tardiness requires a full O(n) pass through the schedule since completion times are cumulative — swapping jobs at position i can shift completion times for all subsequent jobs. There is no shortcut to avoid recomputing the full sum without more complex bookkeeping.

The algorithm terminates when no adjacent swap improves the objective (a local optimum in the swap neighborhood) or when max_iter is reached. This guarantees termination while providing a practical upper bound on runtime.

This is a classic metaheuristic pattern: start from a given solution, define a neighborhood (adjacent swaps), and greedily apply the best neighbor. It won't guarantee global optimality but works well in practice for scheduling problems where EDD (Earliest Due Date) or SPT heuristics provide a good starting point.
key_learnings: Adjacent swap neighborhoods are O(n) in size and natural for permutation problems — each swap is evaluated in O(n) time giving O(n²) per iteration
Greedy steepest-descent (best neighbor) is slower per iteration than first-improvement but makes larger progress steps — trade-off depends on landscape
Tardiness is non-separable: swapping jobs at position i changes completion times for ALL jobs after i, so the full sum must be recomputed
Local optima in swap neighborhoods are not global optima — or-opt, 3-opt, and simulated annealing extend this pattern to escape local minima
For single-machine scheduling, EDD (sort by deadline) minimizes maximum lateness; minimizing total tardiness is NP-hard, making iterative repair practical

numpy.explanation:
The NumPy version replaces the Python loop inside compute_tardiness with vectorized operations. np.cumsum computes all prefix sums (completion times) in one call, and np.maximum(0, ...) with np.sum replaces the inner loop — making tardiness evaluation significantly faster for large n.

Advanced fancy indexing (pt[ord_arr], dl[ord_arr]) reorders both arrays simultaneously without explicit loops, leveraging NumPy's optimized C backend. The outer swap-search loop remains in Python since swaps are sequential and depend on each other's results.

For large schedules (n > 1000), the vectorized tardiness computation provides a meaningful speedup over pure Python since NumPy's cumsum and element-wise ops run in compiled code. The dominant cost shifts from the inner tardiness loop to the O(n) Python swap loop overhead.

This pattern — vectorize the inner tight loop, keep control flow in Python — is a common and effective optimization strategy when full vectorization of the outer structure is impractical due to data dependencies.
key_learnings: np.cumsum is the idiomatic way to compute running totals (completion times) — avoids explicit loops for prefix sums
Fancy indexing (arr[index_array]) reorders arrays without loops and is key to efficient permutation manipulation in NumPy
np.maximum(0, x) is the vectorized equivalent of max(0, x) applied element-wise — standard pattern for hinge/ReLU-like computations
Vectorize the inner computational kernel (tardiness eval) even when the outer search loop stays in Python — often 10-50x speedup on large n
In-place swap with Python tuple unpacking works correctly on numpy int64 scalars, but be careful with numpy array slices (use .copy() if needed)
